concerns.location: plan.md: Statistical Methodology -> 2. Lag Adjustment & Physics-Based Lag
concerns.text: The plan defines L_phys = 6371 / Vsw_mean (minutes) as a heuristic reference, but the formula is dimensionally incorrect for the stated physics. Distance = 60 Re = 60 * 6371 km. Time = Distance / Speed. If Vsw is in km/s, Time (s) = (60 * 6371) / Vsw. To get minutes, divide by 60. The plan's formula simplifies to 6371/Vsw, which implies a distance of only 1 Re (6371 km), not 60 Re. This is a fundamental physics error that invalidates the 'physics-based' comparison metric.

concerns.location: plan.md: Statistical Methodology -> 3. Significance Testing (Permutation Test)
concerns.text: The permutation test strategy (randomly permuting the Ey time series) breaks the temporal autocorrelation structure of Ey. While this breaks the link to Vsw, it creates a null distribution that does not preserve the 'red noise' (autocorrelated) characteristics of the actual magnetotail data. In time-series analysis, a standard permutation test often yields inflated Type I error rates (false positives) because the null distribution is too narrow compared to the autocorrelated data. The plan claims this 'accounts for autocorrelation,' but standard permutation does not; a block permutation or phase-randomization method is required to preserve the spectral properties of the null hypothesis.

The plan specifies 'Block bootstrap (or standard bootstrap)' for confidence intervals. Standard bootstrap is invalid for autocorrelated time series as it assumes independence. The plan must mandate Block Bootstrap (e.g., Moving Block Bootstrap) with a specific block length determination method (e.g., based on the autocorrelation time scale) to be scientifically sound. Leaving 'standard bootstrap' as an option introduces a high risk of underestimating confidence interval width.

The plan treats L_phys as a 'reference value' but uses a formula that is dimensionally inconsistent with the 60 Re assumption (see first concern). If the formula is corrected to (60 * 6371) / (Vsw * 60), the resulting lag will be ~60x larger than currently calculated. This error propagates to the 'lag_difference' metric in SC-002, rendering the comparison between optimal lag and physics-based lag meaningless.
